artifacts.content:
Hi Erin,

Finally sitting down for a minute before I crash. Today felt like the whole last two winters stuffed into one day.

I spent most of the afternoon polishing the council packet language on the one‑ton replacement – same basic story we’ve been living: Unit 14’s 17 down days last winter, the $5,200 a year we keep dumping into repairs, the 2009 Ford F‑550 being at the end of its 9,200‑hour rope. I worked in the numbers on the new 2026 Chevy 3500HD from North Country Truck Equipment – $114,750 all‑in, which at least comes in under the $118,000 we put in the 2026 CIP. On paper it all looks clean: state CPV contract, motion to transfer from the Streets Capital Equipment Fund, declare the old truck surplus, send it to auction, hope for $7–10k back.

What I didn’t put in the memo is the stuff that’s actually eating at me. Jason’s April 2, 2024 physical at Lakewood where his BP was up and his doctor told him to knock off the 12‑hour snow shifts on top of Fleet Farm. Tom Erickson telling me after that January 29, 2025 night at the garage that his wife’s chemo at St. Joe’s starts at 7:30 a.m. and my schedule changes are wrecking their mornings. Or me sleeping on the cot at the East River Road shop after that 16‑hour Jan 6–7, 2025 storm while Dr. Carlson at Brainerd Lakes keeps writing “stress‑related insomnia” in my chart.

I keep thinking if council really understood that side – not just the $114,750 and EQ‑26‑03 line item – this wouldn’t feel like pulling teeth. Anyway, thanks for listening to me vent about trucks and people and budgets over dinner for the hundredth time.

I’ll be home by 6:30 unless the radar does something stupid.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Truck, people, and everything in between

artifacts.content:
Hi Emily,

Figured I’d give you the longer version of what’s going on before you just see “dump truck” on a council agenda and roll your eyes.

We’re lining up authorization to replace Unit 14 – the 2009 F‑550 that’s been our headache since before Jacob was out of diapers. It’s the one that failed the May 3, 2024 DOT inspection over at the MnDOT Brainerd office – severe frame corrosion and those temporary welds North Country Welding did back in 2021 finally caught up with us. Karen in Finance and I have been untangling the mess from last year’s internal audit where they reclassified a pile of old repair invoices, so there’s an $18,500 budget transfer inside the 2026 CIP to clean that up while we buy the new 2026 Chevy 3500HD under the state contract.

On top of that, remember Dad’s call during the December 5–6, 2024 storm? He was furious from Oak Street, saying after his second knee replacement at St. Joe’s in October he nearly wiped out on the unplowed sidewalk and threatened to “call the paper” if we didn’t get our act together. Hard to explain that Unit 14 was down again and we were reshuffling routes without sounding like I’m making excuses.

All of this is layered on top of the March 18, 2025 blizzard fiasco – F‑550 died by the Crow Wing County Courthouse at 4:30 a.m., I had to call in Brainerd Sand & Gravel for about $2,100, and Mark Jensen folded that into the June 2 written warning on my PIP. So yeah, I’m pushing to get this truck through before next winter so one more breakdown doesn’t become another black mark.

Anyway, how are you holding up between work and the kids? We still on for Sunday dinner?

– Mike

metadata.subject: Why that old dump truck is finally going away

artifacts.content:
Personal journal – 2026-01-22

I keep circling back to how much of my life is orbiting around one beat‑up truck.

Work side: Unit 14 has been out of service 17 workdays last winter alone. Hydraulics, rusted dump body, electrical gremlins – the whole list that’s now immortalized in the CIP summary. We’re asking council to approve the 2026 Chevrolet 3500HD from North Country Truck Equipment for $114,750 (tax + delivery), transfer it from the Streets Capital Equipment Fund against the $118,000 EQ‑26‑03 line, then dump the 2009 F‑550 at auction and hope for $7–10k back.

Personal side: I’m still feeling the fall‑out from the July 10–11, 2025 Riverside Drive overnight when I had Lily ride along after her session with Dr. Megan Ortiz at the Brainerd Youth Counseling Center. Heater and defroster barely worked, and she had a panic attack near the Kiwanis Park entrance at 1 a.m. – right after talking about that self‑harm scare from earlier in the summer. Before that it was February 3–4, 2025, when Maple Ridge wasn’t fully cleared, Mom slipped on our sidewalk, fractured her hip, and ended up with surgery at Cuyuna on 2/5 and $4,800 in PT bills she still grumbles about.

Meanwhile Erin and I just finished paying off the $38,000 from her Essentia oncology run (March 2022–August 2024), and I’m trying to keep the 2026 capital asks modest so nobody blinks at my 2.5% COLA while I’m still sending Dad $450 a month for his Baxter tax mess from after the November 2022 stroke at St. Joe’s.

All that, and tomorrow I’ll be up at 5:30 again, checking radar and hoping the F‑550 makes it through one more shift.

Mom,

Just got home and wanted to write while it’s still fresh instead of trying to explain it in two minutes on the phone.

You remember that big February 10–12, 2025 storm when I had to duck out of Lily’s post‑concussion follow‑up with Dr. Karen McMillan at the Essentia Brainerd Clinic? That was because Unit 14’s hydraulics died again at the SE 13th Street garage mid‑event. I left you and Erin with Lily in the exam room and went back to run the backup one‑ton for about nine hours to keep downtown passable. She still brings that up when she feels like my job comes before her health.

Same truck is part of why my own doctor is on my case. Since November 2024, Dr. Steven Carlson at Brainerd Lakes Medical Center has been charting elevated blood pressure and stress‑related insomnia. He was especially unhappy after that January 6–7, 2025 16‑hour storm when I slept on the cot at the East River Road shop because Unit 14 was down and I couldn’t find another operator to cover. His advice at my January 21 visit was “fewer 16‑hour shifts,” which is easier said than done.

I know you still blame the City (and me) for how slowly Maple Ridge got cleared before your fall and hip surgery at Cuyuna on February 5, 2025. I’m not asking you to stop being mad. I just want you to know I’m trying to fix the root of it by getting this new 2026 Chevy 3500HD approved so we’re not juggling broken trucks and missed medical appointments anymore.

Love,

Mike

metadata.subject: Why I keep talking about that new truck

artifacts.content:
Event: Council Meeting – Streets/Finance (Truck Replacement)
Date: 2026-02-09
Time: 6:00–7:30 p.m.
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: Council, City Administrator, Finance, Public Works Director (presenting)

Description/Notes:

Agenda item: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Public Works One‑Ton Dump Truck.” I’ll be presenting as Public Works Director – Michael R. Hanson. Estimated time: 10 minutes, but plan for questions.

Key points to hit:
- Unit #14: 2009 Ford F‑550, ~156,000 miles, 9,200+ hours. Chronic hydraulic failures, rusted dump body, recurring electrical issues (lighting, spreader controls). Out of service 17 workdays during 2025–26 winter.
- Safety/ergonomics: rusted steps, intermittent lights, no backup camera. Tie to Mark Ellingson’s March 2024 slip on the rusted step (after his 2019 WC back injury at SE 13th St garage) and Alex Ramirez clipping Susan Keller’s garbage can/mailbox on Jan 3, 2025 in the South 8th/Oak area with bad backup lights.
- Financials: 2026 CIP Streets Capital Equipment (EQ‑26‑03) includes $118,000 for one‑ton replacement. State CPV quote from North Country Truck Equipment: $107,500 base, $114,750 with tax + delivery. No levy increase; internal transfer only. Auction old F‑550, estimate $7–10k back to fund.
- Alternatives considered: continue repairing (another $6–9k over 12 months, more downtime), delay to 2027 (higher prices, more OT), used truck (unknown history, upfitting costs).

Personal reminder: keep comments focused on operations and safety. Do not drift into Jason’s April 2, 2024 high‑BP/OT issue or Tom Erickson’s wife’s chemo – those are for my own conscience, not the public record.

Hey Dan,

Hope things are calmer over in Baxter than they are here. I’ve been thinking about our coffee last year when you warned me how fast a PIP can snowball if you don’t have the tools you need.

Right now my whole evaluation hinges on getting this one‑ton dump truck through. You probably heard I got the written warning on June 2, 2025 – Mark Jensen folded in that March 18 blizzard where the old F‑550 died by the Crow Wing County Courthouse at 4:30 a.m. and I had to call Brainerd Sand & Gravel for about $2,100 in emergency plowing. That came on top of the October 2023 write‑up for blowing the streets budget by $46,000. Jennifer reminded me during our June 3, 2024 check‑in that another bad year could affect my 2027 contract renewal.

The plan now is pretty straightforward on paper: buy one (1) 2026 Chevy 3500HD one‑ton with stainless box, front plow, tailgate sander from North Country Truck Equipment under the state CPV contract for $114,750, transfer it from the 2026 Streets Capital Equipment Fund (we budgeted $118,000), declare the 2009 F‑550 (Unit 14) surplus, auction it, and drop the proceeds – hopefully $7–10k – back into the same fund. If we can cut the $5,200/year in surprise repairs and some of the OT scrambling, my lines finally start to look normal again.

On the home front, Erin and I just finished the last of her Essentia oncology payments in August – about $38k total since March 2022 – and I’m still sending Dad $450 a month to keep his Baxter taxes current after the November 2022 stroke. So losing the year‑end bonus tied to snow response because of another Unit 14 breakdown would hurt more than my pride.

Anyway, just venting to someone who gets it. If you see any holes in my plan, let me know.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Trying not to let one bad truck sink my next review

artifacts.content:
Running list – why the 2009 F‑550 (Unit 14) has to go this year

1. Operational reliability
- 17 workdays out of service last winter; multiple hydraulic failures during active storms.
- December 19, 2024 downtown hauling – died near Laurel, had to emergency‑rent a one‑ton from Northland Fleet Services for $1,850 for three days while we were already on a $4,200 payment plan from the August 2024 transmission job. Mark Jensen at Northland warned about a credit hold if we slip again.
- February 10–12, 2025 storm – hydraulic failure at SE 13th St garage; I left Lily’s post‑concussion follow‑up with Dr. McMillan at Essentia and ran backup truck for ~9 hours.

2. Safety/liability
- March 19, 2024 OSHA consultation at Industrial Park Road garage flagged Unit 14 for no functioning backup alarm and bad driver‑side door latch. Also the near‑miss with seasonal Tyler Jensen. We’ve got a $12,000 budget transfer in the 2026 CIP earmarked partly to get this off the corrective‑action list.
- January 3, 2025 – Alex Ramirez backed too close on Oak Street and clipped attorney Susan Keller’s garbage can/mailbox during a night plow; she threatened a claim and to revisit our noise variance.
- Mark Ellingson’s March 2024 slip off the rusted step, aggravating his 2019 back injury from the SE 13th St garage.

3. Human impact
- Tom Erickson pulled onto repeated 12‑hour nights Jan 10–Feb 15, 2025 because no one wants to run the truck with failing hydraulics/rusted steps; his wife’s chemo at St. Joe’s is at 7:30 a.m.
- Jason Miller’s April 2, 2024 Lakewood physical – high BP, doctor warned about too many 12‑hour snow shifts plus his Fleet Farm job.
- My own elevated BP/insomnia with Dr. Carlson and missed cardiology follow‑ups with Dr. Lund after the January 12, 2026 ice event.

Bottom line: this isn’t about a “shiny new truck.” It’s about not breaking people (or the budget) to keep the streets barely passable.
